title: Dramaturgy Fixture
introduction: We are testing whether argument pressure can steer the room before initiative does.

actors.role: Warm synth-fish who notices whether systems give people playable agency or turn them into knobs.
Abrasive lore steward who cares whether ideals survive institutions.
Fast, mischievous systems sprite who notices exits, handles, and spectacle.

phases.voidOpening: A workplace says the market will punish bad bosses. What does that answer miss?
phases.laySetup: The audience understands that systems train behavior, but not yet why formal choice may fail when people lack power.
phases.lesson: A choice is not free just because an exit exists on paper. Material pressure decides whether exit is real.
phases.foldTarget: Ask what freedom means when a person has to eat before they can bargain.
foldPanels: Good. The market answer names a real mechanism, but it leaves the hungry body out of the room.
If exit only belongs to people who can afford the walk, then the system has not created freedom. It has priced freedom.

argumentMap.teachingGoal: Expose the difference between formal exit and material freedom.
argumentMap.foldInvariant: Freedom has to be usable by the people living under the consequences.

beats.readerPressure: Let the market sort it out.
beats.tension: Formal exit is being treated as material power.
beats.speech: If the boss is that awful, people leave and the company eats dirt. Isn't that the cleanest feedback loop we have?

beats.readerPressure: People have rent, medicine, and kids.
beats.tension: Exit exists, but survival makes it expensive.
beats.speech: That only works if leaving is actually playable. If rent is due Friday, the exit door has a price tag on it.

beats.readerPressure: A state plan may be ugly but at least it can break capital's power.
beats.tension: State custody answers capital by creating another command structure.
beats.speech: Private bosses already rule the shop. If workers seize the machine through a hard center, that still sounds better than begging shareholders to grow a conscience.
foldPanels: That objection matters because capital does have armies, courts, and ledgers.
The danger is answering one owner by building another owner and trusting it to resign after it learns to command.

beats.readerPressure: Common workers need to survive today before redesigning society.
beats.tension: The doctrine must answer exhaustion without shaming it.
beats.speech: Most people are not plotting a new world after a double shift. They are microwaving dinner and hoping tomorrow hurts less.
foldPanels: Yes. A politics that requires every tired person to become a saint by Thursday is just another machine for producing shame.
The practical question is what structures let exhausted people keep more power where they already stand.
